issues.title: Document sections
issues.author: chris-box
issues.body:
The current order of sections is
> Abstract
> Introduction
> Terminology
> Proposed updates to RFC3819
> Reordering in L2 links
> Resequencing in L2 standards

I think it would make more sense if we described the problem before coming to the solution.
> Abstract
> Introduction
> Terminology
> Reordering in L2 links
> Resequencing in L2 standards
> Experimental results
> Proposed updates to RFC3819

(At this point the results section is a placeholder)

comments.author: gwhiteCL
comments.body: Agreed.  I will go ahead and make that change.

issues.title: Unclear advice
issues.author: chris-box
issues.body:
https://github.com/gwhiteCL/draft-white-intarea-reordering/blob/357bcb86a09ab97b794a03e1c05bc124458b7a14/draft-white-intarea-reordering.xml#L165-L176

The above could do with being clearer.

First, BPS99 is not listed in this document's references. We could use https://dl.acm.org/doi/pdf/10.1109/90.811445 perhaps. It's a very readable paper.

The first paragraph is twenty years old, being unaltered from 3819. It starts from an assumption that we want to eliminate reordering, and it says that sometimes that's not ideal. However the thrust of our draft is that we want links to do less resequencing. I think this paragraph needs amending to fit with its new surrounding context.

The second paragraph says "_Nonetheless, there may exist implementations of network protocols that do not work well in the presence of a significant amount of packet reordering._" In another part of the document we say that IPSEC and L2TP don't like reordering. So we shouldn't say "there may exist", because we know they exist. There may be others too. Hopefully they are rare, and they don't break completely but just degrade.

The final paragraph is succinct and should stay.

comments.author: gwhiteCL
comments.body:
This section is definitely a strawman, and I expect it will evolve significantly over time.  

Based on your notes above, I deleted the paragraph that referenced [BPS99], though I did include that reference in the references section (commented out for now) in case we find it useful to refer to.   I also adjusted the second paragraph to say "there do exist" rather than "there may exist".

That's all that I have time for today, but let's continue to work on improving it.

issues.title: Add a mention of reordering metrics
issues.author: gwhiteCL
issues.body:
[RFC4737](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc4737/) defines these measurements:
* Percentage of reordered packets (4.1)
* Extent of reordering (4.2): maximum distance, in packets, from a reordered packet to the earliest packet received that has a larger sequence number
* Lateness (4.3): how late is the late packet?
* Buffer size needed to resequence (4.4)
* Gaps between reordering events (4.5)
* Runs of in-order packets (4.6)
* n-reordering (5)


Also, a researcher at Colorado State University studied this problem a few years ago:
https://projects-web.engr.colostate.edu/cnrl/Reorder/reorder_papers.html

In particular, this paper seems interesting:
http://www.cnrl.colostate.edu/Reorder/compmetrics_for_ietf.pdf
it provides opinions on the usefulness of the different metrics

We should at least add a mention of these reordering metrics.

issues.title: What should the draft say about L4 awareness?
issues.author: gwhiteCL
issues.body: Different (largely historic) protocols do have out-of-order sensitivities.   Should the draft acknowledge that L2 networks could identify such protocols (by IP protocol /Next Header, or by port number) and selectively resequence?

comments.author: IngJohEricsson
comments.body:
I think that perhaps it be brought up in the draft, but I am not convinced that it is a way forward. Identification of services/protocols that are sensitive to packet reordering is a challenge on its own. How do you from an L2 perspective determine that a VPN tunnel is sensitive to large amounts packet reordering, and as a followup, if said VPN exposes this trait, does it open up a vulnerability? and is it better to upgrade the VPN with better (adaptive?) replay protection ? 
Footnote.. I am not an IPSEC expert so expect large holes in my argumentation

comments.author: gwhiteCL
comments.body: From an architecture and standards viewpoint, I definitely share the concern with trying to build L3+ "intelligence" into L2 protocols.  I could see this as a workaround to address certain problematic cases if they exist, but I would see this as a vendor-proprietary implementation rather than something that gets standardized in any way.  I don't think we want to (for example) register certain port numbers or protocols that are expected to be resequenced by L2 protocols.  I am also not an IPSEC expert, but I recall hearing that some implementations (or maybe some configurations?) are already more tolerant of reordering than others.

comments.author: chris-box
comments.body:
We have choices. I think there are three general approaches we could take.

| Option | Default policy | Exceptions | Policy for the exceptions | Effect on modern implementations | Effect on older implementations |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| One size fits all | Some reordering permitted, up to a point | None | N/A | Slightly improved | Slightly degraded |
| Default strictly in order | Resequence all packets | Some ToS patterns (e.g. L4S or NQB) and/or some L4 ports (e.g. HTTP/3, DNS) | Larger amount of reordering permitted | Improved, if exception matched | No change |
| Default permissive | Larger amount of reordering permitted | IPSEC, PPP, some L2TP | Resequence all packets | Improved | For the listed exceptions, no change. For pre-RACK TCP, slightly degraded. |

I am not a fan of the middle one, as it would create a burden on future protocol evolution that they always to have match the exceptions if they want to benefit.

I would be happy with either the first or third ones. The advantages of Default Permissive are that it has a lower operational barrier to deployment, and might deliver better latency. Its downside is the need for inspection and branching.

issues.title: Fragmentation
issues.author: IngJohEricsson
issues.body: At the INTAREA session the issue with fragmentation was raised. Do I interpret it like they envision a case where IP packets are fragmented in L2 and then concatenated in the wrong way ?

comments.author: gwhiteCL
comments.body: I didn't interpret it that way.  I think they were referring to the receiving IP endpoint being unable to handle receiving fragments out of order.

comments.author: dibakard
comments.body: What are the fragments ? In WiFi, layer-2 typically doesnt fragment packets received from upper layers.

comments.author: IngJohEricsson
comments.body:
I don't see that 5G RAN would fragment  IP packets either. And besides, I have some real bad experience with fragmented IPv4 packets in 4G/5G networks. We stumbled on these issues with too large packets from video coders in an industrial project. 
And IPv6 fragmentation is limited to only in NICs on the server side I think ?

comments.author: gwhiteCL
comments.body: Nor does DOCSIS.  But, I think the concern is that a sender (or maybe a VPN tunnel encap.) could fragment IP packets.  Then if the L2 link reorders those fragments, the IP fragment reassembly at the receiver (or tunnel decap.) might have issues.

comments.author: IngJohEricsson
comments.body:
Yes, that sounds like a plausible explanation. 
It is actually the GTP-U overhead that caused the segmentation and we had all sorts of issues with crap video before we realized that we needed to turn down the MTU to avoid IPv4 fragmentation. 
Also TCP Linux has MTU discovery built in and there is also an MTU discover RFC for RTP/UDP

comments.author: gwhiteCL
comments.body:
There isn't a protocol issue that I can see with IP fragmentation.  It seems to fully support out-of-order arrivals, so maybe there are some implementations that are broken (or were broken in the past)?

I've definitely heard multiple times that IP fragmentation should be avoided if at all possible.

issues.title: IP Translators
issues.author: gwhiteCL
issues.body: At Intarea IETF124, it was mentioned that translators like CG-NAT might be sensitive to out-of-order arrivals.

issues.title: IPsec sensitivity to reordering
issues.author: gwhiteCL
issues.body:
From Yoav Nir <ynir.ietf@gmail.com>

> My 2 cents about the subject is that IPsec (that’s the one correct capitalization) is sensitive to re-ordering. Packets are numbered, and you’re not supposed to forward the decrypted packet #19 to the network until you’ve already forwarded packet #18, so that internal protocols that are sensitive to packet re-ordering may work.  In practice most implementations keep a sliding window (typically 64 packets) and will decrypt and forward anything within that sliding window.  But then I have not worked on an actual IPsec implementation in 8 years, so people in the working group might have better insights. 


From Tero Kivinen <kivinen@iki.fi>

> EESP and g-ikev2 are changing how the replay windows work, mostly to get rid of them, as if you forward packets with very different paths, there might be huge differences in the packet delay times, thus some of them might be received out of the replay window.

comments.author: gwhiteCL
comments.body: Per Lorenzo Colitti (during TSVWG @ IETF124):  Linux IPsec by default uses a 32 packet window for replay protection, but has a "bitmap" feature which can be enabled that extends this to 4k.

pulls.title: Shorten abstract
pulls.author: chris-box
pulls.body:
https://authors.ietf.org/required-content says "_An abstract will typically be around 50-150 words in length. An abstract that is much shorter or longer is generally not acceptable._" The existing abstract is 227 words. This is my proposal for a shorter abstract.

I've also taken the liberty of redefining the intention of the document. By the time it is finished I hope we'll have good data and recommendations developed, so that the final version will contain these new recommendations. However if you think we should retain the existing smaller ambition of "_to promote an analysis and discussion of the sensitivity of modern IETF protocols to out-of-order delivery, and to potentially develop new information_" then we can do that.

comments.author: chris-box
comments.body: Definitely, I agree it can be moved to a suitable place in the introduction.

reviews.author: gwhiteCL
reviews.body: Agreed that we need to shorten the abstract.  But, I'd like to not lose the explanation about the lack of knowledge of higher layer protocol contexts. I think that is an important point that even for protocols that are intolerant of reordering, the existing mechanisms aren't always beneficial.  Can we move that material into the Introduction rather than deleting it?

pulls.title: Improve multi-link wording
pulls.author: chris-box
pulls.body: In my experience it's nearly always the case that links have non-identical throughputs or latencies. So the text can be less tentative.

pulls.title: LTE/5G updates
pulls.author: chris-box
pulls.body:
Miscellaneous tweaks and improvements to this section.

I have chosen to delete the "low latency video" text because I have not heard such a use case in our network. As far as I know all video is delivered over the best effort internet bearer which uses AM.

comments.author: gwhiteCL
comments.body: These updates look good to me.  Since that text came from @IngJohEricsson I'll wait for him to check that he's ok with the changes.

pulls.title: Edits in proposed guidance
pulls.author: gwhiteCL
pulls.body: per the comments in #8, I made a few changes.
